content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 16 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 213–214 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_016_page_001.jpg`, `DEU_016_page_002.jpg` | **LXX Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/DEU_016_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `DEU_016_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### Passover — Remember the Night of Liberation (vv. 1–8)

**1** Observe the month of Aviv and celebrate the Passover of the LORD your God, because it was in the month of Aviv that the LORD your God brought you out of Egypt by night. **2** Sacrifice as your Passover offering to the LORD your God an animal from your flock or your herd at the place the LORD will choose as a dwelling for his Name. **3** Do not eat it with leavened bread. For seven days eat unleavened bread — the bread of hardship — because you left Egypt in such haste. Remember the day you left Egypt all the days of your life, for the rest of your life. **4** Let no leavening be found in all your territory for seven days, and do not let any of the meat you sacrifice on the evening of the first day remain until morning.

**5** You must not sacrifice the Passover in just any of the towns the LORD your God is giving you. **6** Sacrifice it only at the place the LORD your God will choose as a dwelling for his Name — sacrifice it at evening as the sun goes down, the time of your departure from Egypt. **7** Roast it and eat it at the place the LORD your God will choose. Then in the morning you may return to your tents. **8** For six days eat unleavened bread, and on the seventh day hold a solemn assembly for the LORD your God — do no work on that day.

---

### The Festival of Weeks — Celebrate the Harvest (vv. 9–12)

**9** Count off seven weeks from the time you first put the sickle to the standing grain. **10** Then celebrate the Festival of Weeks to the LORD your God by giving a freewill offering proportionate to the blessing the LORD your God has given you. **11** Celebrate in the presence of the LORD your God — you, your sons and daughters, your male and female servants, the Levites in your towns, and the foreigners, fatherless, and widows among you — at the place the LORD your God will choose as a dwelling for his Name. **12** Remember that you were slaves in Egypt, and follow these decrees carefully.

---

### The Festival of Booths — Rejoice in the Fullness (vv. 13–15)

**13** Celebrate the Festival of Booths for seven days after you have gathered in the produce of your threshing floor and your winepress. **14** Rejoice at your festival — you, your sons and daughters, your male and female servants, and the Levites, the foreigners, the fatherless, and the widows who live in your towns. **15** For seven days celebrate the festival to the LORD your God at the place the LORD will choose. The LORD your God will bless you in all your harvest and in all the work of your hands, and your joy will be complete.

---

### Three Times a Year — Appear Before the LORD (vv. 16–17)

**16** Three times a year all your men must appear before the LORD your God at the place he will choose: at the Festival of Unleavened Bread, the Festival of Weeks, and the Festival of Booths. No one should appear before the LORD empty-handed. **17** Every man shall bring a gift in proportion to the way the LORD your God has blessed him.

---

### Appoint Judges — Pursue Justice (vv. 18–20)

**18** Appoint judges and officials for each of your tribes in every town the LORD your God is giving you. They must judge the people fairly. **19** Do not twist justice. Do not show partiality. Do not accept a bribe — for a bribe blinds the eyes of the wise and corrupts the words of the innocent. **20** Pursue justice — only justice — so that you may live and possess the land the LORD your God is giving you.

---

### No Sacred Poles, No Standing Stones (vv. 21–22)

**21** Do not set up any wooden pole — a sacred Asherah pole — beside the altar you build for the LORD your God. **22** And do not erect a standing stone — the LORD your God hates these.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v. 1 — "month of Aviv"**: Aviv means "green ears" — the first month of spring when barley ripens. PNEUMA retains "Aviv" rather than the later Babylonian name "Nisan" because the text uses the agricultural calendar. The Exodus is tethered to the rhythms of creation.

**v. 3 — "the bread of hardship"**: Hebrew *lechem oni* — bread of affliction, poverty, or suffering. Dynamic: "bread of hardship." The unleavened bread is not merely a ritual artifact; it is an edible memory of slavery. Every bite is meant to trigger embodied recollection: this is what it tasted like to be desperate.

**v. 3 — "in such haste"**: Hebrew *chipazon* — hurrying, urgency. The Exodus departure was not a dignified procession but a panicked flight. Unleavened bread encodes that urgency.

**v. 6 — "the time of your departure from Egypt"**: PNEUMA renders this with interpretive clarity. The evening sacrifice at the central sanctuary is deliberately timed to the hour of the original Exodus departure — worship reenacts history.

**vv. 9–12 — Festival of Weeks**: Later called Pentecost (fifty days after Passover). The offering is a "freewill offering proportionate to blessing" — not a fixed amount but a responsive generosity calibrated to what God has given. Wealth itself becomes a reason for greater worship.

**v. 11 — The full guest list**: PNEUMA notes that the Festival of Weeks guest list — sons, daughters, servants, Levites, foreigners, orphans, widows — is the most inclusive list in the chapter. The festival of firstfruits is not a private family event but a community-wide celebration that deliberately seats the vulnerable at the table.

**v. 13 — "Festival of Booths"**: Hebrew *Sukkot* — booths, shelters, temporary structures. For seven days Israelites were to live in improvised shelters, recalling the wilderness journey. PNEUMA uses the English name throughout while preserving "Booths" in the title to indicate the nature of the celebration.

**v. 15 — "your joy will be complete"**: Hebrew *v'hayita ach sameach* — and you shall be only joyful, utterly joyful. PNEUMA renders this with emphasis. The festivals are not solemn obligations but commanded celebrations. Joy is the destination; gratitude for harvest is the vehicle.

**v. 19 — "a bribe blinds the eyes of the wise"**: Hebrew *hashochad ya'aveir einei chakhamim* — bribery blinds even the wise. The text does not say it blinds the foolish (who are already compromised) but the wise — those most capable of fairness. Power and intelligence offer no protection against corruption once money speaks.

**v. 19 — "corrupts the words of the innocent"**: Hebrew *y'salem divrei tzaddikim* — distorts, overturns the words of the righteous. A bribe does not merely bend a corrupt judge — it makes the honest seem dishonest, the righteous seem wrong. This is systemic injustice.

**v. 20 — "Pursue justice — only justice"**: Hebrew *tzedek tzedek tirdof* — justice, justice, you shall pursue. The doubled word *tzedek* is emphatic — PNEUMA renders the intensity: "only justice." The word "pursue" implies active, relentless chase — not passive waiting for justice to happen.

**vv. 21–22 — Asherah poles and standing stones**: These were objects of Canaanite fertility worship. Their prohibition directly follows the justice commands — suggesting a connection between idolatry and the corruption of courts. When the people worship rightly, they judge rightly. — *PNEUMA*
